upperAir: The OAK sounding shows a strong low-level temperature inversion: surface temp 15.6°C at 3m rising to 20.2°C at 850mb (1515m), indicating a pronounced inversion capping thermals well below 1500m — likely effective thermal ceiling around 300–400m AGL given the shallow boundary layer heights (275–430m) seen in model data. The lapse rate surface-to-850mb is only -3.04°C/km, far below dry adiabatic (9.8°C/km), confirming a very stable airmass with extremely limited thermal development. Winds at 850mb are light from 190° at 2 kts, backing from the surface NW 8 kts, with 700mb showing 350° at 2 kts — minimal shear aloft but a notable direction discontinuity between surface and mid-levels.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area SF region. However, METARs are notable: KSFO reporting 290° at 21 kts gusting 27 kts (peak gust 28 kts at 2229Z), indicating strong late-afternoon NW flow through the Golden Gate corridor. KHAF reporting 330–350° at 13–18 kts gusting 22–25 kts through the evening — strong northerly/NNW flow at Half Moon Bay directly impacts Mussel Rock approach. These surface observations indicate the sea breeze/NW flow is punchy and gusty along the coast.

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W/NW-facing ridge
sites.howItWorks: Pure ridge-soaring site driven by onshore marine sea breeze. Ideal wind is W to NW at 10–20 kts at ridge height. Too light and there's no lift; too strong or northerly and rotor/turbulence risk increases significantly. KHAF is the key proximity METAR.
sites.todaySetup: Morning hours look very light and variable — 10m winds are only 1.9–4.5 kts from 239–275° between 09:00–11:00, well below the 8–10 kts typically needed for reliable ridge soaring. Wind direction clocks NW through midday and afternoon, reaching 7–8.4 kts at 10m by 14:00–16:00, which is closer to the usable range. However, by 17:00 the model shows 12.7 kts at 10m with 80m winds hitting 19.1 kts — a significant gradient indicating increasing instability and potential rotor development. The KHAF METARs are the real concern: by late afternoon they're showing 330–350° at 13–18 kts gusting 22–25 kts, which translates to a northerly component that creates turbulent, cross-ridge flow at Mussel Rock. BL height stays capped at 275–330m all day with zero CAPE — stable but unhelpful for thermals. The mid-afternoon window (13:00–15:00) at 7–8 kts NW (293–306°) is the only plausible soaring window, but the rapidly building KHAF winds suggest conditions deteriorate sharply after 15:00.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 2–4.5 kts from SW-W (239–275°), too light for reliable ridge lift. Variable direction and 82% cloud cover at 08:00 adds to the flat, borderline feel. Not worth launching.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind builds to 6–7 kts from 279–306°, approaching the usable NW window. 13:00 shows 8.5 kts at 80m — potentially soarable for experienced pilots, but direction is drifting NNW which can make the ridge angle awkward.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2–5 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds increase to 7.6–12.7 kts at 10m with 80m winds hitting 14–19 kts by 17:00, and KHAF METARs confirm 15–18G25 kts from 330–350°. This northerly-dominant, gusty flow creates rotor risk and cross-ridge turbulence. Conditions deteriorating — land before 4 PM.

verdict.hazards: Morning too light; afternoon NNW gust cycle 15–25 kts per KHAF METARs creates rotor risk. Narrow usable window. Rapidly building winds after 3 PM.

sites.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — lower training bowl launch, SW-facing
sites.howItWorks: The 300 ft launch is a training hill relying on light valley thermal breezes or gentle upslope flow. Ideal for light SW to W winds 5–12 kts. Very limited altitude to work with — thermals must be accessible close to the hill. More sensitive to overdevelopment and sea breeze timing than the upper launches.
sites.todaySetup: Morning winds are light and variable (3–4.6 kts, shifting from 300° to 242° to 307°) with near-zero BL height at 08:00 (40m) — no usable thermal or ridge lift. By 11:00–12:00 BL climbs to 245–320m, but at 300 ft launch elevation this is barely above launch height, leaving minimal working altitude. Winds build from the W at 5–7.7 kts between 12:00–15:00 at 294°, which is a reasonable direction but the 10m-to-80m wind gradient shows flow increasing with height (7.6–9.5 kts at 80m by 14:00). With zero CAPE and an extremely stable sounding (lapse rate -3.04°C/km), thermals will be weak, short-lived, and punchy — not smooth soaring air. Cloud cover surges to 93–100% from 12:00 onward, further killing thermal potential. This is a tough day for the training hill — usable window is narrow and the reward is low.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: BL height only 40–245m, winds 3–4.6 kts and variable in direction. No thermal or ridge lift available. Ground-handling practice only.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds settle around 272–294° at 5–7.7 kts — on-slope and potentially launchable. BL reaches 320–370m by 13:00, giving a thin but workable thermal window. Sky overcast at 93–98% by noon kills thermal quality.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2–5 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds increase to 7.7–9.4 kts at 10m with 80m up to 11.1 kts from 294–313°. 100% cloud cover by 15:00–17:00. Flow becoming gusty and punchy for a training hill with limited escape options.

verdict.hazards: Very stable airmass limits thermal height and quality. Cloud cover >93% from noon kills thermals. Afternoon winds increase toward gusty. Minimal altitude buffer above launch.

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft launch uses a combination of thermal and valley breeze lift, ideal when the BL is climbing through 300–500m and W to NW winds are 8–15 kts. More forgiving than the 300 ft in terms of glide options, but still needs working thermals to stay aloft meaningfully.
sites.todaySetup: Morning is very light and confused — 2–3 kts from varying directions (266°, 325°, 222°, 349°) through 11:00 AM, and BL height only reaches 35–155m by 10:00. Not usable. The 12:00–13:00 window is marginal: winds pick up to 2.2–5.7 kts from 297–276° and BL climbs to 335m. By 14:00–16:00, winds are 6.2–7.3 kts from 303–316° — proper W-NW direction — but BL height maxes at 405–430m, only slightly above the 600 ft (183m) launch elevation, leaving limited thermal ceiling. CAPE is essentially zero all day (one 50-unit blip at 15:00), confirming the stable sounding. Cloud cover is 92–100% from noon to 17:00, blocking solar heating and killing thermals. The afternoon flow does provide ridge lift potential from the NW (311–326°), but gustiness is increasing — 80m winds reach 10.5 kts by 17:00. A narrow window exists around 13:00–15:00 for experienced pilots.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 2–3 kts in random directions, BL only 35–245m. No usable lift at any level. Stay on the ground.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind comes around to W-NW at 5.7–6.7 kts by 13:00–14:00, BL reaching 335–360m. Marginal thermal window but sky overcast 92–98% — expect weak, unreliable lift. Ridge soaring from NW flow may be the primary mechanism.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2–5 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 6.2–9 kts from 311–326° — solidly NW and strengthening. Potential ridge lift on the west-facing launch. 100% overcast limits thermals. Flow approaching gusty by 17:00 (80m winds 10.5 kts).

verdict.hazards: Deep overcast from noon suppresses thermals. Morning wind too light and variable. Afternoon flow increases toward gusty with 80m-to-10m gradient. Stable sounding means any thermals will be sharp and punchy.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.howItWorks: The top launch at 1750 ft is the site's XC and soaring workhorse. It needs thermals to climb through the BL or sufficient NW ridge lift. Ideal in moderate W-NW flow 10–18 kts with a BL topping 500–800m. The extra altitude gives a long glide and time to find thermals. Wind direction from SW-W-NW is on-ramp; NE or S is cross/tail.
sites.todaySetup: The summit launch shows a confused and unstable-looking morning: 08:00 winds from 190° (southerly) at 5.5 kts, shifting to 101° (easterly) at 09:00 — these are tail/cross winds and launch would be inappropriate. By 10:00, a weak 117° (ESE) at 2.2 kts persists. Winds finally come around to N-NW (29°, 17°, 277°) between 11:00–13:00 but are very light (2–4.4 kts). The real NW flow arrives by 14:00–16:00 at 5.2–6.9 kts from 315–327°, with 80m winds reaching 7.3 kts. This is on the light side for the summit launch but usable. BL height peaks around 355–380m — well below the 1750 ft (533m) launch elevation, meaning pilots are launching above the thermal ceiling. This is a critical problem: there are no thermals to sustain flight above the inversion, and ridge lift from 5–7 kts NW is on the weak side for a meaningful flight. Cloud cover is 91–100% from noon onward. By 17:00 winds reach 7.8 kts at 10m, 9.5 kts at 80m — increasing but still modest. Overall a very unfavorable day for the top launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds from S/SE/E (190°–29°) — cross or tailwind at the NW-facing summit launch. Do not launch. BL barely 35–265m. Zero usable conditions.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind direction improves to N-NW-W between 12:00–14:00 but only 2.6–5.2 kts — likely below flyable threshold for the summit. BL height at 345–360m is below launch elevation (533m), so pilots are above the thermal ceiling.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2–5 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NW flow solidifies at 6.5–7.8 kts from 315–327° by 15:00–17:00 with 80m winds up to 9.5 kts. Potentially flyable for a sled run in light ridge lift, but 100% overcast and BL well below launch height means no thermal support. Winds building toward marginal-strong by end of day.

verdict.hazards: Morning S/SE winds are unflyable for NW-facing launch. BL height below launch elevation all day — no thermal ceiling above launch. 100% overcast from noon. Only viable as a short sled run in weak NW ridge lift. Not worth the hike for most pilots.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday March 22 looks similar or worse. BL height drops to just 45m at 08:00 suggesting marine layer intrusion. Winds remain W at 6–9.7 kts but 100% cloud cover through midday at 80m level suggests thick marine layer. Afternoon may clear partially (33% at 14:00) with 7.8–9.7 kts W-NW — potentially soarable in the 2–4 PM window if marine layer retreats, but moisture signals are concerning. Monitor closely.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday morning shows a wild 80m wind spike: 11.5 kts at 08:00 from 333° with 10m only 4.8 kts — strong northerly shear aloft. Morning is a no-go. Midday through afternoon (12:00–16:00) looks cleaner: 5.2–7.8 kts from 283–307°, BL reaching 390–470m, and mostly clear skies (1–15% cloud). Better thermal potential than today. Afternoon could be a decent P3 day.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday morning 600 ft launch is a hard no — 14.3 kts from 020° (northerly, cross/tail for W-facing launch) with 80m at 21.6 kts. Very dangerous. By noon conditions normalize to 3.8–6.9 kts from 299–325° with BL 420–435m and clear skies — a reasonable afternoon window. Pilots should wait until at least 11 AM and verify winds have fully transitioned.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday morning summit also shows strong N-NNE flow (9–9.9 kts at 10m, 13.5–19.2 kts at 80m from 020–027°) — unflyable and potentially dangerous. By midday the flow shifts to NW at 6–7 kts with BL climbing to 405–425m and mostly clear. A solid afternoon window 12:00–16:00 if morning shear clears. P3 pilots check early then reconvene at noon.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Monday March 23 morning starts clear with 6.8 kts at 267° (due W) — a promising sign. Winds hold 5.7–6.7 kts from 274–282° through 14:00 before 100% cloud cover arrives. The W direction and consistent 6 kts offers stable ridge soaring in the morning to early afternoon. Cleaner and more consistent than today. Looks like a good Mussel Rock day if the marine layer doesn't fill in early.

day2.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday shows light, clean conditions: 3–6.2 kts from 261–323°, BL climbing to 375–640m by afternoon with nearly clear skies (2–7% cloud through 14:00). BL height reaching 640m is notably higher than today — better thermal quality possible. Afternoon may be a good training hill day with 6.2 kts at 309° at 14:00.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday 600 ft looks similar to 300 ft with light morning winds and a building afternoon: 6.2 kts at 309° by 14:00, BL 640m, clear skies. The high BL relative to launch elevation means real thermal altitude gain is possible. Best day of the three-day window for Ed Levin thermal flying. P2/P3 opportunity in the afternoon.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday summit looks promising: morning winds light from 319° (NW) at 6.2 kts, shifting to calm ESE by 10:00, then building W-NW at 6.2 kts from 309° by 14:00. BL hits 640m — above the lower launches and approaching the summit elevation (533m), meaning thermals could work near or above launch. Best summit day of the outlook if skies stay mostly clear (7% cloud at noon). Strong P3 day potential.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: No data beyond March 23 in the current dataset. Extend the forecast if planning beyond Monday.

day3.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day3.analysis: No data beyond March 23. Monday already looks like the best day of the period — plan accordingly.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: No data beyond March 23. Monday afternoon is the highlighted opportunity for thermal soaring at Ed Levin.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: No data beyond March 23. Monday is the standout summit day in this window — mark it on the calendar.

watchlist: 1) KHAF METARs showing 330–350° at 13–18G25 kts in the evening — monitor Saturday afternoon at Mussel Rock for rapidly building northerly gust cycles that can arrive faster than forecast. 2) Ed Levin 600 ft Sunday morning: the 14.3 kts northerly at 10m and 21.6 kts at 80m is a serious shear hazard — do not launch until that transition is fully confirmed on the ground. 3) BL height at Ed Levin 1750 ft today is below launch elevation all day — pilots launching above the inversion have no thermal support and will be committed to a sled run in potentially weak-and-variable air. 4) Monday March 23 BL heights (~640m) are notably deeper than the weekend — this could indicate the start of a warming trend worth tracking for soaring potential later in the week.
tldr: Today is a tough day across all sites — a strongly inverted, stable airmass caps thermals well below Ed Levin's upper launches, and coastal winds are either too light in the morning or too strong/northerly by afternoon at Mussel Rock. If you're itching to fly, the best window is a 1–3 PM sled run or ridge scratch at Mussel Rock or Ed Levin 600 ft for P3 pilots only. Save your energy for Monday — BL heights hit 640m, skies are clear, and it looks like the best thermal day of the weekend.
